title: Sand and Water Play Table
summary: A kid-height play table with two drop-in plastic tubs — one sand, one water — on a slatted storage shelf base. Buy the tubs first; the table is built around them.
description:
The design brief for a sand-and-water table is short: hold two plastic tubs at toddler height, survive being outside, and let the tubs lift out for dumping and hosing. This build does it with a 1x6 top frame divided into two tub-sized openings, 1x3 legs, and a slatted 1x3 shelf underneath for buckets and molds.

The first item on the shopping list is the most important instruction: buy the tubs FIRST. The frame openings are sized so the tubs hang by their rims, and plastic tubs come in every size but the one a plan assumes. Get your tubs, measure the rims and the bodies, and adjust the frame dimensions before cutting — the listed cuts fit the source's tubs and are a starting point, not gospel.

The fastener note in the source is real-world experience talking: outdoors, use screws, not nails. Wet wood swells and shrinks with every rain cycle, and that movement walks nails out of their holes a little at a time — on a kids' table, a proud nail head is a cut waiting to happen. Self-tapping exterior screws stay put. And pre-drill the 1x3s; they split near ends.

Everything gets sanded and every edge gets rounded, because the users are three years old and made of elbows.

tools.note: All straight crosscuts. A circular saw works fine.
Screws, not nails, for anything living outdoors — swelling wood pushes nails out over time.
Pilot holes through the 1x3s — they split out near ends.

materials.name: Plastic tubs
materials.note: BUY THESE FIRST and size the frame openings to their rims. The cut list assumes the source's tubs.

materials.name: 1x3, 8 ft
materials.note: Legs, shelf frame, and shelf slats.

materials.name: 1x6, 12 ft
materials.note: Top frame. Have the store cut it to 4 ft and 8 ft for transport — plan the crosscuts so all the pieces still come out.

cutList.note: Splits the top into the two tub bays — position it to YOUR tubs' rim sizes.

steps.title: Build the top frame
steps.body:
Assemble the 1x6 top frame — sides, ends, and the center divider that creates the two tub bays. Exterior glue at every joint and the 2" screws, pilot-drilled.

Before driving a single screw, set your actual tubs in the dry-fit frame: rims should catch on the frame all around, bodies should hang free. Move the divider until both tubs fit. The tubs are the spec; the cut list is the suggestion.

steps.title: Build the shelf frame
steps.body: Build the 1x3 shelf frame to the same outer footprint as the top frame — same construction, lighter stock. This one carries buckets and shovels, not sand and water; it doesn't need the 1x6 beef.

steps.title: Legs, then join the frames
steps.body:
Flip the TOP frame face-down on a flat surface and screw a leg into each corner with the 1-1/4" screws and glue. Then slide the shelf frame down over the legs to its height and screw it to all four.

Do this on a genuinely flat surface — a table this small shows every millimeter of leg mismatch as a wobble, and toddlers lean on everything.

steps.title: Slat the shelf, sand, done
steps.body:
Screw the twelve shelf slats across the shelf frame with the 1-1/4" screws, even gaps — the gaps drain rain and shed sand instead of collecting both.

Sand everything and round over every edge and corner a kid can find (they will find all of them). Drop in the tubs: sand one side, water the other, chaos both. The tubs lift out for dumping — which is the entire maintenance plan.

images.alt: Kid-height wooden play table with two inset plastic tubs for sand and water
